text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic right colectomy, visualizing the abdominal cavity and manipulating the colon following a previous colonic resection.
The importance of three-D CT angiography reconstruction and ICG fluorescence.
Laparoscopy is considered the gold standard treatment for colorectal cancer.
The evolution of technology and techniques has pushed the limits ever forward, even if some challenging settings are still debated.

An eighty-two-year-old man with a BMI of thirty-eight, diabetic, cardiopathic and oral anticoagulant therapy with chronic renal failure was admitted to our emergency department for colonic perforation after undergoing a colonoscopy that revealed a stenosing ascending colon cancer.

Past surgical history was characterized by an open cholecystectomy performed through a wide subcostal incision and a laparoscopic left colectomy for a PT-N-I-B adenocarcinoma with high IMA ligation, complicated by an ostomotic leak, that was treated conservatively.
CT scan revealed a bulky, locally advanced, eight-centimeter tumor of the ascending colon with localized perforation, without evidence of free fluid, air or metastatic disease.
Stage was at T-four because of suspect infiltration of the surrounding tissues and of the transverse colon.
CT scan confirmed the high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery occurred during the previous colonic surgery.
This case has several challenges that must be taken into account to plan the best operative strategy.
This was an old morbidly obese man with several comorbidities and a history of abdominal surgeries performed by open and laparoscopic approach with multiple complications.
A possible compromised colonic blood supply due to the previous high tie of the IMA
the emergency setting the locally advanced T-IV cancer.
Surgical strategy was also challenged.
Should a subtotal colectomy and iliorectal anastomosis be considered in such an elderly patient, even in case of questionable functional results?
In a patient with peripheral vascular disease and a previous IMA ligation, would a segmental colectomy be a safe option?
The surgical approach was also in question.
Would such a patient benefit from laparoscopy?
And if so, would laparoscopy be feasible?
The first decision was to try to avoid surgery in an emergency setting.
The patient was given parenteral nutrition, IV fluids, antibiotics and electrolytes.
Surgery was then scheduled as an elective procedure about one week later.
What we knew about the patient's anatomy was that he had a left-sided colocolic anastomosis, but we did not know if the splenic flexure had previously been mobilized or not.

Since a high tie of the IMA was performed during the previous colonic surgery, a careful preoperative study of the blood supply of the remaining colon was performed with a three-dimensional CT scan with vascular reconstruction.
The scheduled operation was a fully laparoscopic right colectomy.
The laparoscopic approach was chosen in order to reduce surgical trauma.
Patient was put in the modified Lloyd-Davies position and the operating theatre was set up as shown.
The surgeon positions a 10mm Hasson trocar and two 5mm trocars in the abdominal wall, preparing for the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon encounters extensive omental adhesions from a previous open cholecystectomy and carefully divides them using an ultrasonic scalpel.
The surgeon lifts the omentum and transverse colon to expose the operative field, providing a clear view for the laparoscopic right colectomy.
The laparoscopic view reveals infiltration of the omentum, indicating possible tumor involvement.
The surgeon identifies and exposes the ileocolic pedicle, revealing its anatomical position within the operative field.
The outlines of the ileocolic vessels and the superior mesenteric artery are clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs medial to lateral mobilization by opening the peritoneum below the heliocolic vessels, facilitating the separation of tissue layers in the operative field.
During the mobilization process, the surgeon carefully identifies and preserves the duodenum, ensuring it remains intact while manipulating surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues dissection along the root of the mesentery, following the anatomical pathway established during previous mobilization steps.
The surgeon isolates and divides the heliocolic vessels at their origin, clearly separating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the coloepiploic ligament, aiding in the mobilization of the transverse colon.
The surgeon performs a partial omentectomy, excising the infiltrated area of the omentum to ensure complete removal of affected tissue.
The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the hepatic flexure, ascending colon, and cecum, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon isolates and ligates the right colic artery, completing the vascular control necessary for the resection.
The surgeon manages the middle colic vessels, which are the only remaining vascular supply, carefully isolating and addressing them to ensure proper blood flow management.
The surgeon isolates the middle colic artery and ligates the right branch, while preserving the main branch, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the mesocolon, extending the dissection up to the transverse colon, ensuring separation of the mesocolon from surrounding structures.

The surgeon assesses the blood supply of the transverse colon and terminal ileum using ICG fluorescence, ensuring adequate perfusion before transecting these sections with a laparoscopic linear stapler.
The surgical specimen is placed into an endobag and retrieved through a Pfannenstiel incision, with the incision protected by a wound retractor.
The video displays the excised specimen of the colon, showing both its anterior and posterior views, highlighting the anatomical features and surgical margins.
The bulky T4 tumor is clearly visible in the surgical field, showing its size and position relative to surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs an ileocolonic side-to-side intracorporeal anastomosis, connecting the ileum and colon using surgical techniques visible within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs enterotomies on the anti-mesenteric side of the ileum and transverse colon, creating openings in these sections of the bowel.
The surgeon carefully aligns the staple line to avoid including both mesenteries during the closure of the anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the enterotomy using a double layer running PDS suture, ensuring secure closure of the incision site.
After an uneventful postoperative course, the patient was discharged on postoperative day four.
Pathology report confirmed a grade three perforated T four N zero R zero tumor of the ascending colon.
At two years follow up, the patient is disease free and in good clinical conditions.
Complex cases with history of previous colon resections require a tailored surgical strategy and accurate planning.
Choosing the right timing to surgery is key to optimize patient conditions and lower perioperative morbidity.
CT angiography is a useful tool when planning the best operative strategy in non-standard vascular anatomy.
Laparoscopy can be beneficial even in very complex and previously operated patients.
Intraoperative vascular supply assessment with ICG fluorescence can support the plan-tailored strategy.
In conclusion, minimally invasive surgery can be considered an option even in elderly, morbidly obese and clinically complex patients.
Preoperative optimization and a tailored surgical strategy should always be considered.
ICG fluorescence angiography can be useful in checking blood supply, particularly in patients with previous colonic resections.
